title: Handling Duplicate Numbers in Binary Search

statement.markdown: Your task is to find the index of a target number in a sorted array with possible repeated values. The function should return the smallest index where the target number appears. If the target number does not appear, return -1.
statement.inputFormat: Input is a number n, followed by n space-separated integers in the array, and a target number.
statement.outputFormat: Return the smallest index of the target number.
statement.notes: Binary search needs some modifications to handle arrays with duplicate values.

examples.explanation: The target number 4 appears for the first time at index 2.
The target number 2 appears for the first time at index 1.

hints.text: Check whether left and right pointers are correctly updated in each iteration.

editorial.title: Optimal Solution: Modified Binary Search
editorial.intuition: We can use a standard binary search approach but adjust our left and right boundaries in case we encounter repeated values.
editorial.bruteForce: Naive brute-force search can work for small inputs but will be inefficient for large arrays.
editorial.optimalSolution: Modify the binary search loop to continue searching in the left half if the right half contains equal elements.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: We can show that this modified binary search still has a logarithmic time complexity but provides the correct answer.
editorial.dryRun: Let's perform a dry run to see how the modified binary search would behave with repeated values.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly updating left and right pointers.

functionSignature.cpp: bool find(int n, int target, vector<int>& arr);
functionSignature.java: public boolean find(int n, int target, int[] nums);
functionSignature.python: def find(self, n, target, nums: list[int]) -> bool;
